Facts.text: John II's reign as Catholic Pope ends, upon his death
Visigothic King Reccared summons the Third Council of Toledo, where he accepts the Catholic faith. Before this he and the Visigoths were Arian Christians
Saint Boniface IV's reign as Catholic Pope ends
St Benedict II's reign as Catholic Pope ends after 11 months, upon his death
University of Salamanca granted a royal charter by Alfonso IX. Third oldest university still operating and the oldest Hispanic.
Treaty of Brétigny signed by English & French, ending the first phase of the Hundred Years' War
Pierre Dugua de Monts reaches Cap La Hève (La Have) on the Nova Scotia coast, on his way to establish Arcadia, the first successful permanent settlement in New France
Dutch Nassau fleet reaches port of Callao in Peru intending to ransack the annual Spanish silver ship convoy, misses them by five days
William Coddington founds Newport, Rhode Island
Lady Blanche Arundell, surrenders Wardour Castle, which she had led the defense of against a siege by Parliamentarian forces
Michelangiolo dei Conti succeeds Pope Clement XI, as Innocent XIII
France and Bavaria sign the Covenant of Nymphenburg
Richard Brinsley Sheridan's comedy play "School for Scandal" premieres in London
Only known deaths by hailstones in the US occur in Winnsboro, South Carolina

First US prison reform society formed, the Philadelphia
Society for Alleviating the Miseries of Public Prisons led by Dr. Benjamin Rush
British Captain George Vancouver sights and names Mt Rainier, Washington
US establishes the military draft
US passes first foreign aid bill, authorizing up to $50,000 to assist victims of the Caracas, Venezuela earthquake on March 26
Greek War of Independence: Greeks defeat the Turks in Gravia
"Home! Sweet Home!" by John Howard Payne is 1st sung in London
Prussia, Austria and Russia sign classified accord about Belgium
Alexander Wolcott patents Photographic Process
Versailles to Paris train catches fire; 50 die
First major battle of the Mexican–American War fought at Palo Alto, Texas
Scottish inventor Robert Thompson patents his pneumatic tyre in the US
Second London protocol signed reaffirming Danish federation control of independent Duchies of Holstein, Lauenburg and Schleswig
Richmond, Virginia, is named the capital of the Confederacy in the US
Valley Campaign: Federals repulsed at Battle of McDowell, Virginia
Confederación Granadina becomes Estados Unidos de Colombia
Actions at Stony Creek/Nottoway bridge, Virginia (Drewry's Bluff)
Atlanta Campaign: Severe fighting near Dalton
Battle of Spotsylvania Court House begins
British-US treaty ends Alabama dispute
First Westminster Dog Show is held
George Selden files the first patent for a gasoline-powered automobile
David Belasco's "La Belle Russe" premieres in NYC
Sarah Ann Henley survives 76-m jump from Clifton Bridge in Bristol, England
Jacob's Pharmacy in Atlanta sells the first Coca-Cola (contained cocaine)
China cedes Taiwan to Japan under the Treaty of Shimonoseki
The first games of the Italian Football League are played
The Irish Literary Theatre in Dublin opens
A British appointed commission estimates today that some 1,250,000 Indians have died after a severe drought, lasting since 1899
In their long-delayed AL home opener, Boston defeats Philadelphia 12-4
Mount Pelée on the French overseas island of Martinique erupts, wiping out the city of Saint-Pierre, killing 30,000 people and leaving only two survivors
Union of Unions organizes In Russia under the chairmanship of Paul Miliukov and joins liberal groups demanding parliamentary government and universal suffrage
Philadelphia A's pitcher Albert Bender is deployed to left field in 6th inning and responds with 2 HRs in A's 11-4 win over Boston Americans at Huntington Avenue Grounds, Boston
Boston Doves pitcher "Big Jeff" Pfeffer no-hits Cincinnati Reds, 6-0 at South End Grounds, Boston
American Albert Raines runs world record marathon (2:46:04.6) in NYC
British runner Henry Barrett runs world record marathon (2:42:31) in the Polytechnic Marathon in London
Film and television production and distribution studio Paramount Pictures is founded
German munitions bunker in Fort Douaumont explodes killing 679 German soldiers
Irishmen Eamon Kent, Michael Mallin, Con Colbert and Sean Houston are executed by British authorities following the Easter Rising at Kilmainham Gaol, Dublin
Appingedam soccer team forms
First transatlantic flight by a Navy seaplane takes off
Sweden abolishes capital punishment
Memel territories given to Lithuania
Workers at Werkspoor in Amsterdam strike against 3rd wage cut
French colonial army beats Rifkabylen in Morocco
A. Philip Randolph organizes Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters
Fire breaks out in Fenway Park, home of Boston Red Sox baseball team
Jan Mayen Island, 500 km NNE of Iceland, is incorporated into Norway
Operette "Land of Smiles" premieres in London
MLB Cincinnati Reds catcher Ernie Lombardi doubles in 6th, 7th, 8th & 9th in 15-4 victory over Phillies, in game one of a double header at Philadelphia's Baker Bowl
Jockey Ralph Neves unexpectedly revived after being declared dead after a fall. His wife faints when he returns to the track.
Stravinsky's "Dumbarton Oaks" premieres in Washington, D.C.
Archaeologist Basil Brown begins excavating at Sutton Hoo in Suffolk, goes on to find a royal 7th century Anglo-Saxon burial ship - the greatest archeological discovery on British soil
German Q-ship Pinguin sinks in Indian Ocean
1st twilight game in 24 years, the Dodgers top Giants 7-6 raising $60,000 for Navy Relief Fund
Aircraft carrier USS Lexington sunk by Japanese air attack in Coral Sea
German summer offensive opens in Crimea
Admiral Cunningham of British fleet: "Sink, burn & destroy; let nothing pass"
33 communist resistance fighter sentenced to death
Canadian troops move into Amsterdam
Chinese counterattack at Tsjangte, supports by 14th air fleet
Estonian school girls Aili Jõgi and Ageeda Paavel blow up the Soviet memorial that preceded the Bronze Soldier in Tallinn
MLB Boston Red Sox shortstop Johnny Pesky scores 6 runs in 14-10 win over visiting Chicago White Sox
West German constitution approved
Tollund Man, an early Iron Age bog body found hanged as a human sacrifice, discovered by men digging for peat in Jutland, Denmark
Dacron men's suits introduced
US performs atmospheric nuclear test at Enewetak Atoll
WIPB TV channel 49 in Muncie, IN (PBS) begins broadcasting
First shot-put throw over 60 feet (18.29 meters) by American Parry O'Brien in Los Angeles, California
John Osborne's "Look Back in Anger" premieres in London
Three-deck Nile River excursion steamer springs a leak, panicking passengers who capsize the ship; 200 drown just yards from shore
1st performance of Ferde Grofé's "San Francisco Suite", with Grofé conducting, in San Francisco
USSR & Cuba resume diplomatic relations
First practical seawater conversion plant begins operations in Freeport, Texas
1st Atlas Centaur Launch
London trolley buses (electrically powered buses using overhead supply) go out of service
Randy Matson throws the first shot put over 70 feet, achieving a distance of 70 feet 7 inches
Last game at old Busch stadium, St Louis Card lose 10-5 to San Francisco
The Philippine province of Davao is split into three: Davao del Norte, Davao del Sur, and Davao Oriental
Jim (Catfish) Hunter of Oakland pitches perfect game vs Twins (4-0)
Pulitzer prize awarded to William Styron (Confessions of Nat Turner)
Cambodia recognizes the German Democratic Republic (East Germany)
Construction workers break up an anti-war rally in NYC's Wall Street
The Beatles release 12th, and final, studio album, "Let It Be", in conjunction with the film of the same name
Thousands of students protest against the Vietnam War following the Kent State University shootings in Ohio
Sabena aircraft at Lod International Airport, Tel Aviv, captured by Palestinians
50 MPH speed limit in Britain lifted
The rollercoaster Revolution (roller coaster), the first steel coaster with a vertical flip, opens at Six Flags Magic Mountain
ABC TV airs "Stars Salute Israel at 30"
Radio Shack releases TRSDOS 2.3
1st non-stop trans-North American balloon flight departs Fort Baker, California. Maxie Anderson and son Kristian pilot the Kitty Hawk for five days.
Sabres take only 15 shots, Islanders 22, in a playoff game
World Health Organization announces smallpox has been eradicated
Ron Davis pitches 10th consecutive strike out, 1 short of record
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
Thames Barrier to stop flooding in London officially completed
The Soviet Union announces it will not participate in Los Angeles Summer Olympics in retaliation for the American boycott of the 1980 Moscow Olympics
France performs nuclear test at Mururoa Atoll
Gary Hart quits US democratic presidential race due to affair with Donna Rice
The Loughgall ambush: The SAS kill 8 IRA members and 1 civilian, in Loughgall, Northern Ireland
Amateur referees work NJ Devils-Boston Bruin playoff games, as NHL referees walk-off, due to a restraining order brought by Devils
US space shuttle STS-30 lands
Cuyahoga County voters approve sin tax to build Cleveland Gateway
Reindependence Day of Estonia
ABC Masters Bowling Tournament won by Phil Ware
Sixteen-year-old Keron Thomas disguises himself as a motorman and takes a NYC subway train with 2,000 passengers on a three-hour ride
500th commentary by Andy Rooney on 60 Minutes
All-female pro baseball team Colorado Silver Bullets play their first game
Erling Kagge becomes the first person to complete the Three Poles Challenge by foot, reaching the North Pole, South Pole, and the summit of Mount Everest
Ernesto Perez Balladares elected president of Panama
Jose Maria Figueres becomes president of Costa Rica
South Africa's Constitutional Assembly adopts permanent post-apartheid constitution
Nancy Mace becomes the first female cadet to graduate from The Citadel military college (South Carolina)
The new Canadian War Museum opens, in commemoration of the 60th anniversary of V-E Day
Barrow AFC wins English FA Trophy at Wembley, makes them only team to win it at both Old & New Wembley stadiums
Last piece of Yankee Stadium falls in the Bronx, New York, marking the end of the two year demolition process
33 people are killed after an overloaded bus falls into a river in Himachal Pradesh, India
NFL Draft: South Carolina outside linebacker Jadeveon Clowney first pick by Houston Texans
Ebola outbreak declared in north-west Democratic Republic of Congo with 2 confirmed cases and 17 deaths
Largest-ever wave at 23.8m recorded in the South Hemisphere at Campbell Island by New Zealand scientists
Paris St-Germain seals the French domestic football treble with a 2-0 win over 3rd Div Les Herbiers in the French Cup Final at the Stade de France
Seattle Mariners MLB left-hander James Paxton hurls a no-hitter in a 5-0 win over the Blue Jays in Toronto
Facebook co-founder Chris Hughes argues that Facebook should be split up and regulated in "New York Times" Op-Ed essay
Governor of Georgia Brian Kemp signs new abortion law, the so-called "heart-beat bill" (comes into effect Jan 2020)
Iran announces it will stop complying with parts of the 2015 Iran nuclear deal
Pakistani Christian woman Asia Bibi who spent eight years in prison on death row for blasphemy arrives in Canada
Singapore passes sweeping "anti-fake news" laws bill
Thailand's general elections official results show opposition Pheu Thai Party won most seats but still unable to form governmen, due to rules favoring the military
UK goes a week without using a coal-fired power station for first time in 137 years due to use of more renewable energy
11-year-old Brazilian skateboarder Gui Khury creates a world record by performing the first 1080-degree turn on a vertical ramp
Two US White House staffers test positive for COVID-19 and three members of the coronavirus task force go into self-isolation after possible exposure to the virus
Bombings outside a school in Kabul, Afghanistan, kill at least 50 people, mostly teenage girls, amid growing fears about US military withdrawal
Indian Medical Association calls for a national lockdown, criticizing President Modi's government's response as recorded COVID-19 deaths pass 4,000 a day
British actor Ncuti Gatwa is named as the first Black actor to play the Doctor in "Doctor Who" as the 14th Doctor, replacing Jodie Whittaker in the long-running BBC sci-fi drama
Dutch driver, Max Verstappen (24) wins the 1st Miami Grand Prix Formula One motor race at the Miami International Autodrome in Miami Gardens, Florida
Dutch runner Jo Schoonbroodt (71) breaks Canadian Ed Whitlock's 2004 record for fastest marathon by person over 70 by 29 seconds (2:54:19) at Maasmarathon of Visé in Belboom, Belgium
Pro-Beijing loyalist and sole candidate, John Lee elected Chief Executive of Hong Kong by the city's Election Committee
Thai government announces it will give away one million cannabis plants to households to mark new rule allowing people to grow it at home
US First Lady Jill Biden visits Ukraine's First Lady Olena Zelenska at a school in Uzhhorod, Ukraine
Death toll from flash floods and landslides in Kalehe, Congo, passes 400 with President Felix Tshisekedi declaring a national day of mourning for the victims
Car manufacturer Toyota posts record profit of 4.94 trillion yen ($31.9 billion) and sales, selling 10.3 million cars including 3.7 million hybrids
New York has more millionaires than any other city in the world, one in 24, with 744 centi-millionaires worth more than 100 million and 60 billionaires according to new ranking
Jack Cade's Rebellion: Kentishmen revolt begins against KingHenry VIand they march towards London
Hungarian King Bethlen Gabor and Holy Roman EmperorFerdinand IIsign the Treaty of Vienna, confirming the Peace of Nikolsburg agreed in 1621
English Lord ProtectorOliver Cromwellrefuses the English crown
English Parliament proclaimsCharles IIKing of England and invites him to return
First installment ofHans Christian Andersen's "Fairy Tales" is published by C. A. Reitzel in Copenhagen, Denmark
American abolitionistJohn Brownholds a secret antislavery convention in Canada
David Edward Hughes' paper on the idea for a microphone is read before the Royal Society of London by Thomas Henry Huxley
Henry Morton Stanleysigns the first of many contracts with the Congolian monarch
Ernest Rutherfordpublishes his discovery of two different kinds of radiation (Alpha and Beta Particles)
CanadianTommy Burnsretains his world heavyweight boxing title after beatingPhiladelphia Jack O'Brienon points in 20 rounds in Los Angeles, California
Arthur Honegger's orchestral tribute to steam locomotives "Pacifica 231" premieres
NY Giants pitcherCarl Hubbellno-hits Pittsburgh Pirates, 11-0 at the Polo Grounds, NYC
Soviet government contractAlbert KahnAssociates architectural firm to design the USSR's 1st tractor plant
Mahatma Gandhibegins a 21-day fast in protest against British oppression in India
A movement among Cardinal players to protest its 1st meeting withJackie Robinson& the Dodgers is aborted by a talk from owner Sam Breadon
Polish resistance fighterWitold Pilecki, who had volunteered to be imprisoned in Auschwitz to gain information about the Holocaust, is arrested by Polish communist police
President of the Republic of ChinaChiang Kai-shekasks the US for weapons
South Vietnamese President,Ngô Đình Diệm, arrives in the United States on a state visit
Classic Hammer horror film "Dracula" is released, starringChristopher Leeas the eponymous vampire alongsidePeter Cushingand directed by Terence Fisher
US VPRichard Nixonis shoved, stoned, booed and spat upon by protesters in Peru
Little Caesars Pizza is founded byMike Ilitchand his wife Marian Ilitch in Garden City, Michigan
Alan Shepardreceives NASA Distinguished Service Medal, Washington
Oskar Schindlerand his wife Emilie Schindler are honored for saving 1200 Jews during WWII, in a ceremony on the Avenue of the Righteous, Jerusalem
First James Bond film, "Dr. No," starringSean Connery, premieres in the US
U.S. PresidentJohn F. Kennedyoffers Israel assistance against aggression from its neighbors
Only HR ever hit out of Baltimore's Memorial Park, by Oriole outfielderFrank Robinson
Muhammad Aliis indicted for refusing induction in US Army
PopePaul VIpublishes the Sacra Ritum Congregatio, dividing the Congregation of Rites into two different congregations: one for Divine Worship and another for the Causes of the Saints
Ernie Banksfills in for Cubs managerr Whitey Lockman who is ejected during the game, technically becoming baseball's 1st African American manager
Wounded KneeOccupation ends after 10 weeks as 200 Oglala Lakota of the American Indian Movement surrender the South Dakota hamlet
Canadian government ofPierre Trudeaufalls
The Curerelease their debut album "Three Imaginary Boys" (Boys Don't Cry in US, Australia)
Minnesota TwinsKirby Puckettdebuts with 4 singles
François Mitterrandelected President of France
Mike Tysoncrashes his $183,000 Bentley on Varick St in New York City
Paul McCartneyreleases remake of "Ferry Cross the Mersey" in aid of those affected by the Hillsborough disaster, which claimed the lives of 96 Liverpool football fans
CIA directorWilliam H. Websterannounces his retirement
Lennox Lewisbeats Tony Tucker in 12 for heavyweight boxing title
President Clintonannounces US will no longer repatriate boat people
NY YankeeDwight Goodenwins his 1st AL game beating Tigers 10-3
A new Northern Ireland Executive is formed withIan Paisley(Democratic Unionist Party) as First Minister andMartin McGuinness(Sinn Féin) as Deputy First Minister
Dmitry MedvedevappointsVladimir Putinas Russian Prime Minister
Rock for the Rainforest benefit concert held at Carnegie Hall, NYC; performers include:Sting,James Taylor,Billy Joel,Brian Wilson, Chris Botti, Feist, Roberto Alagna, and Natalie Clein
The Tradition Senior Men's Golf, Shoal Creek G&CC:Tom Lehmanwins second of 3 Champions Tour majors with par on 2nd playoff hole against Australian Peter Senior
Dmitry Medvedevis confirmed as Russian Prime Minister by the State Duma, after being nominated byVladimir Putin
Alex Fergusonannounces his retirement as Manchester United's manager at the end of the season
Former Italian Prime MinisterSilvio Berlusconireceives a four year prison sentence for fraud
The world's oldest astrolabe (mariner's navigation tool) from circa 1498 is found near Al Hallaniyah Island, Oman, from a Portuguese shipwreck of explorerVasco da Gama
Sadiq Khan(L) is elected Mayor of London, becomes the 1st Muslim mayor of a major Western city
President Trumppulls the US out of the multilateral Iran nuclear deal
Tesla chief executive TeslaElon Muskhosts "Saturday Night Live" in the US
Disney live-action film musical "The Little Mermaid" starring Halle Bailey andMelissa McCarthy, directed by Robb Marshall, premieres in the US
Cardinal Robert Prevost (69) is elected as the 267th pope of the Catholic church, the first American to hold the office takes the name ofPope Leo XIV
Ship from Bordeaux carrying the plague, lands in Melcombe Regis (now Weymouth), Dorset. The beginning of the Terrible Pestilence (Black Death) in England.
Parliament of Worms installs edict againstMartin Luther
Spanish explorerHernando de Sotoand his expedition are the first Europeans to discover the Mississippi River
Edward George Honey first proposes the idea of a moment of silence to commemorate The Armistice of World War I, leads to the creation of Remembrance Day

V-E Day: World War II ends in Europe after Germany's GeneralWilhelm Keitelformally signs unconditional surrender to the Allies represented by the United States, the UK, France and the Soviet Union in Berlin, Germany signs an unconditional surrender
US PresidentEisenhowerorders National Guard out of Central High School, Little Rock, Arkansas
Joan I, Countess of Auvergne and Boulogne, Queen of France by marriage to King John II (1350-60), born in Chateau d'Argilly, France (d. 1360)
Frederick I, Margrave of Brandenburg-Ansbach (1486-1536), born in Ansbach, Bavaria, Germany (d. 1536)

Saint Peter Canisius, Dutch Jesuit priest, born in Nijmegen, Habsburg Netherlands (d. 1597)

Charles Beauclerk, 1st Duke of St Albans, English soldier, born in London (d. 1726)

Mikhail Kamensky, Russian field marshal, born in Russian Empire (d. 1809)

Johann Baptist Gansbacher, Austrian composer, born in Sterzing, County of Tyrol, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1844)
Jean Vianney, French Catholic priest, and eventual saint, born in Dardilly, Lyonnais, Kingdom of France (d. 1859)
Thomas Hancock, British engineer, founded British rubber industry, born in Malborough, Wilshire (d. 1865)
Joseph Napoleon Ney, French politician, born in Paris (d. 1857)

Mikhail Bakunin, Russian revolutionary anarchist, born in Pryamukhino, Tver Governorate of the Russian Empire (d. 1876)

George Bruce Malleson, English officer in India and author, born in Wimbledon, London (d. 1898)

Sharbel Makhluf, Lebanese Maronite monk, born in Bekaa Kafra, North Governorate, Lebanon (d. 1898)

Bryan Morel Thomas, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Milledgeville, Georgia (d. 1905)
Adolphe-Basile Routhier, French Canadian lyricist ("O! Canada"), born in Saint-Placide, Quebec (d. 1920)
Emil Christian Hansen, Danish physiologist, born in Ribe, Denmark (d. 1909)
Hermann Gradener, German composer, born in Kiel, Germany (d. 1929)
Oscar Hammerstein, German-American cigar maker, opera and theater impresario, composer, and playwright, born in Stettin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1919)
Ross Barnes, American baseball infielder (3×NA/NL batting champion 1872, 73, 76; 4×NA/NL runs scored leader; 4×NA/NL hits leader; Boston Red Stockings), born in Mount Morris, New York (d. 1915)

Dan Brouthers, American Baseball HOF first baseman (first great MLB slugger; 4×NL batting champion; Buffalo Bisons, Detroit Wolverines, Boston Beaneaters, Brooklyn Grooms), born in Sylvan Lake, New York (d. 1932)

Heinrich Berté [Bettelheim], Slovak-Austrian opera composer (Das Dreimäderlhaus, aka Blossom Time), born in Galgócz, Hungary (d. 1924)

Johan Jensen, Danish engineer and mathematician who worked on the Riemann hypothesis, born in Nakskov, Denmark (d. 1925)

Jef van Hoof, Flemish composer, born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1959)

Edmund Wilson, American literary critic and writer (Patriotic Gore), born in Red Bank, New Jersey (d. 1972)
Fulton J. Sheen, America bishop (Life is Worth Living), born in El Paso, Illinois (d. 1979)
James H. "Dutch" Kindelberger, American aerospace pioneer who headed the team that designed the P-51 Mustang, born in Wheeling, West Virginia (d. 1962)

Vera Chapman, English writer and Tolkien Society founder, born in Bournemouth, England (d. 1996)
Arthur Q Bryan, American voice actor, comedian and radio personality (Beulah, The Devil Bat), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1959)
Friedrich August von Hayek, Austrian-British economist (Road to Serfdom, 1974 Nobel Prize for Economics ), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1992)[1]
Frits van Hall, Dutch sculptor (Monument Indië-Nederland (originally known as Van Heutsz monument), 1935), and WWII resistance fighter, born near Semarang, Dutch East Indies (d. 1945)

Fernandel [Fernand Contandin], French actor (The Little World of Don Camillo), born in Marseille, Bouches-du-Rhône, France (d. 1971)
John Snagge, British newsreader and commentator for the BBC (Oxford-Cambridge Boat Race), born in London (d. 1996)
(Ernest) "Red" Nichols, American jazz cornetist, composer and jazz bandleader (His Five Pennies - "Ida, Sweet as Apple Cider"; "Bugler's Lament"), born in Ogden, Utah (d. 1965)[1]
Inglis Gundry, British composer, musicologist and novelist, born in Wimbledon, London (d. 2000)
David Van Vactor, American classical composer (Chaconne), born in Plymouth, Indiana (d. 1994)
Arturo de Córdova [Arturo García Rodríguez], Mexican actor (Medal for Benny), born in Merida, Yucatan, Mexico (d. 1973)
John Graham, Jr. American architect (Space Needle - Seattle), born in Seattle, Washington (d. 1991)
Laura Liddell, English actress (Shakespeare Wallah), born in Barrow-in-Furness, England (d. 1992)
Mary Lou Williams (née Scruggs), American jazz pianist, arranger (Duke Ellington, Benny Goodman), composer (Zodiac Suite; Mary Lou's Mass), and philanthropist, born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 1981)
George Woodcock, Canadian writer of political biography and history (Anarchism: A History of Libertarian Ideas and Movements), born in Winnipeg, Manitoba (d. 1995)
Gertrud Fussenegger, Austrian writer (Mohrenlegende), born in Pilsen, Austria-HUngary (d. 2009)
Sid James [Solomon Joel Cohen], South African-born British character and comedy actor (Hancock's Half Hour, Carry On), born in Johannesburg, Transvaal Province (d. 1976)

Romain Gary [Roman Kacew], Lithuanian-French writer, also published as Émile Ajar (Les racines du ciel; La vie devant soi), diplomat, and screenwriter (The Longest Day), born in Vilnius, Russian Empire (d. 1980)
John Archer [Ralph Bowman], American actor (Destination: Moon, White Heat, Blue Hawaii), born in Osceola, Nebraska (d. 1999)
João Havelange, Brazilian industrialist and FIFA President 1974-98, born in Rio de Janeiro (d. 2016)
Swami Chinmayananda [Balakrishnan Menon], Indian spiritualist and master of Vedanta philosophy, born in Kerala, India (d. 1993)
Nan Wynn [Masha Vatz], American radio and big-band singer (Rudy Vallée; Teddy Wilson), actress, and singing voice for Rita Hayworth, born in Johnstown, Pennsylvania (d. 1971)[1]

Lex Barker, American actor (Tarzan of the Apes; Treasure of the Silver Lake), born in Rye, New York (d. 1973)
Soltan Hajibeyov, Azerbaijani composer and People's Artist of the USSR, born in Shusha, Azerbaijan Democratic Republic (d. 1974)
Maurice Cranston, English philosopher and political scientist (John Locke biographies), born in Harringay, England (d. 1993)
Saul Bass, American graphic designer of logos and film titles, born in New York City (d. 1996)

Tom of Finland [Touko Laaksonen], Finnish fetish artist, born in Helsinki (d. 1991)
Graham Leonard, Bishop of London, most senior Anglican to convert to Catholicism since the reformation, born in London (d. 2010)
Jack Laird, American screenwriter, producer, and director (Ben Casey, Night Gallery, Kojak), born in Monrovia, California (d. 1991)
